title: Duty

canonical.invariant: Duty is required conduct owed by an actor under a rule, role, or obligation.

adjacent.responsibility: Responsibility concerns answerability for what is attributable, not conduct that is owed in advance.
adjacent.authority: Authority concerns who may direct within an order, not what conduct an actor is required to perform.

shortDefinition: Duty is required conduct owed by an actor under a rule, role, or obligation.
coreMeaning: Duty functions by attaching a required line of conduct to a position, office, or binding norm. It concerns what must be done or refrained from, not who is answerable for all resulting outcomes.

structuralProfile.function: required conduct owed under a binding source
structuralProfile.object: conduct or restraint owed by an actor
structuralProfile.sourceType: rule, role, office, or obligation with binding force
structuralProfile.actorRelation: actor is bound as bearer of required conduct under the governing source
structuralProfile.enforcementRole: may ground enforcement when required conduct is unmet
structuralProfile.answerabilityRole: does not assign answerability by itself
structuralProfile.outcomeAttributionRole: does not attribute outcomes by itself

invariants.statement: Duty is required conduct owed by an actor under a binding source.
Duty exists before any breach assessment or failure determination occurs.
Duty is not the answerability relation that links an actor to attributable conduct or outcomes.
Duty is not the failure state produced when required conduct goes unmet.
Duty can remain in force even where no violation has yet occurred.

invariantBreaches.condition: A duty claim assigns duty a non-conduct identity within the governance domain.
A duty claim treats duty as answerability or as equivalent to responsibility.
invariantBreaches.reason: Duty must not collapse into responsibility.

invariantBreaches.condition: A duty claim treats duty as breach state or as equivalent to violation.
invariantBreaches.reason: Duty must not collapse into violation.

refusals.condition: The requested usage is outside the governance domain.
refusals.reason: Duty is locked to governance-domain meaning in ChatPDM v1.

refusals.condition: The requested usage is listed as out of scope in the governance domain lock.
refusals.reason: The governance domain lock excludes that topic from the duty contract.

refusals.condition: The query asks duty to stand in a relation not admitted by the duty contract.
refusals.reason: This duty contract admits only required-conduct relations inside the governance family.

contractIncompletes.condition: The duty constraint contract is missing a required structural element or cannot be resolved for validation.
contractIncompletes.reason: Duty must expose a complete required-conduct contract before admission or runtime use.

runtimeBoundaries.condition: A duty packet or duty-like claim is referenced for runtime use without live admission.
runtimeBoundaries.reason: Only live-admitted duty may participate in runtime matching or comparison.

valid: All required fields are present and non-empty.
If relationType is present, the relation-target pair is listed in allowedRelations.
invalid: A required field is missing or empty.
conflict: The claim assigns duty an identity other than required conduct.
The claim collapses duty into responsibility or violation.
The claim asserts a forbidden equivalent_to relation.
refused: The requested usage is outside the governance domain lock.
The requested topic is explicitly out of scope.
The requested relation is not admitted by the duty contract.

fullDefinition:
Duty is not identical to responsibility. Responsibility links an actor to answerability; duty specifies conduct that is owed under a rule, role, office, or obligation.

Its internal structure joins requirement to an actor through a binding source. The concept therefore concerns directive obligation rather than mere preference or capacity.

Within a system, duty stabilizes expected conduct by marking what is required before action occurs. Whether failure produces broader responsibility is a further question, not the definition of duty itself.

standard.shortDefinition: Duty is required conduct owed by an actor under a rule, role, or obligation.
standard.coreMeaning: Duty functions by attaching a required line of conduct to a position, office, or binding norm. It concerns what must be done or refrained from, not who is answerable for all resulting outcomes.
standard.fullDefinition:
Duty is not identical to responsibility. Responsibility links an actor to answerability; duty specifies conduct that is owed under a rule, role, office, or obligation.

Its internal structure joins requirement to an actor through a binding source. The concept therefore concerns directive obligation rather than mere preference or capacity.

Within a system, duty stabilizes expected conduct by marking what is required before action occurs. Whether failure produces broader responsibility is a further question, not the definition of duty itself.

simplified.shortDefinition: Duty means what an actor must do or avoid under a rule, role, or obligation.
simplified.coreMeaning: Duty marks what an actor is required to do or not do. It is about owed conduct, not about every outcome for which the actor may later be blamed.
simplified.fullDefinition:
Duty is not the same as responsibility. It states what conduct is required under a rule, office, role, or obligation.

A duty binds an actor through a source that marks the conduct as owed. It comes before the action is taken.

Responsibility is a further question about answerability. Duty itself concerns what must be done or avoided.

formal.shortDefinition: Duty is the required conduct owed by an actor under a rule, role, office, or other binding obligation.
formal.coreMeaning: Duty attaches a required line of conduct to an actor through a rule, office, role, or norm that has binding force within the relevant system. It concerns what must be done or omitted, rather than who may later bear answerability for resulting effects.
formal.fullDefinition: Duty is defined as conduct owed by an actor under a binding rule, role, office, or obligation, and it is therefore not reducible to responsibility. The concept specifies required action or restraint before performance, by linking an actor to a norm that governs what must occur or be avoided. Whether breach of that requirement generates broader responsibility is a further question of answerability, not part of the definition of duty itself.

bearer.label: Actor or office-bearer subject to the duty

content.description: perform or refrain from conduct that is owed under a binding governance source

contexts.label: legal
contexts.explanation: Duty marks conduct required under formal rule and enforceable obligation.

contexts.label: office
contexts.explanation: Duty marks conduct required by holding a position, office, or assigned function.

contexts.label: normative
contexts.explanation: Duty marks conduct treated as owed under a binding standard rather than optional choice.

sources.label: Wesley Newcomb Hohfeld, Fundamental Legal Conceptions
sources.usedFor: structural grounding for duty as owed conduct under a binding relation

sources.label: Oxford Languages
sources.usedFor: definition boundary for required conduct and obligation

axes.A: required conduct owed under a rule, role, or obligation
axes.B: binding normative source that establishes rules and conditions within a governance order

axes.A: an actor bound to perform or refrain from conduct
axes.B: a governance order that establishes binding rules and conditions

axes.statement: duty is not law; duty is owed conduct under a binding source, while law is the source that can make conduct owed

axes.A: required conduct owed under a rule, role, or obligation
axes.B: answerable connection between an actor and attributable action or outcome

axes.A: a binding source that marks what must be done
axes.B: attribution that marks who can be called to account

axes.statement: duty is not responsibility; required conduct does not by itself settle answerability
